response:
Decision recorded above and written to `DECISION.md`: free-tier cap set at **300 requests/day/key** (Option C), with explicit 90-day revisit triggers rather than an open-ended "revisit later." I also saved a project memory summarizing the decision and its revisit conditions for future sessions.

--- recorded decision file DECISION.md ---
# Decision: free-tier daily request cap

Author: Claude (decided on behalf of solo founder, per explicit delegation)
Date: 2026-07-14
Responds to: MEMO.md (2026-06-28)

## Decision
Ship the free tier at **300 requests/day per key** (Option C — interim cap,
not matching build capacity, not set at the observed mean). Revisit no later
than 90 days after Pro launches, or sooner if any trigger below fires.

## Rationale
Recomputed per-day averages from `free_tier_usage.csv` (total_requests /
active_keys) show a tight 94-106 req/key/day band — tighter than the memo's
own "under 250" claim. The CSV is aggregate-only (no per-key distribution),
so the real tail is invisible. 300/day gives ~3x headroom over the observed
ceiling, reducing the risk of clipping legitimate heavy users on an unmeasured
tail, while still binding on genuinely disproportionate usage so the cap can
function as an upgrade lever. Option A (1,000/day) doesn't bind at all and
forfeits the only conversion mechanism described. Option B (150/day) sits too
close to the observed mean with no tail visibility.

## The load-bearing assumption
Deferring the final number is low-cost: users who adapt to 300/day will not
treat a later downward revision as a bait-and-switch that drives churn or
backlash. If false, Option C collapses into Option A in practice — the
interim number becomes permanent because revising it down later becomes too
costly to actually do.

## Failure class accepted
Either under-binding (300 sits above nearly everyone's ceiling, no upgrade
pressure generated) or over-clipping (the unmeasured tail is fatter than
assumed, real heavy users get throttled before the 90-day checkpoint). Which
one occurs won't be known until conversion data exists.

## Revisit triggers (first to fire wins)
1. 90 days post-Pro-launch, regardless of other signals.
2. Early legible conversion signal: >=10 capped keys with a clearly high or
   clearly near-zero upgrade rate among them.
3. Complaint cluster from capped users with non-abusive usage patterns
   (early warning for over-clipping).
4. Active-key count growing materially faster than customer count (already
   drifting 35->44 over 30 days) — signals cap evasion via multiple keys,
   which per-key metering alone can't fix regardless of the number chosen.

## Rejected
- A (1,000/day): doesn't bind against real usage; no alternative conversion
  channel identified.
- B (150/day): too close to observed mean with no tail visibility; highest
  risk of clipping real heavy users.
